questions.question:
A complete n-ary tree is a tree in which each node has n children or no children. Let I be the number of internal nodes and L be the number of leaves in a complete n-ary tree. If n = 4 and I = 41, what is the value of L?

A. 121
B. 122
C. 123
D. 124
questions.to_find: Number of leaves L in a complete 4-ary tree with 41 internal nodes

understand.plain: A complete n-ary tree is one where every non-leaf node has exactly n children. We need to find how many leaf nodes exist given the number of internal nodes and the branching factor. There is a well-known formula relating internal nodes and leaves in such trees.

keywords.explain: A rooted tree where every internal (non-leaf) node has exactly n children, and all leaves have no children.
keywords.example: A complete binary tree (n=2) with 3 internal nodes has 4 leaves.

keywords.explain: A node that has at least one child (i.e., it is not a leaf).
keywords.example: In a tree with root having two children, the root is an internal node.

keywords.explain: A node with no children; it is a terminal node in the tree.
keywords.example: The bottommost nodes in a tree with no further branches are leaves.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='200' cy='30' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>I</text><line x1='200' y1='48' x2='80' y2='100' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='48' x2='147' y2='100' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='48' x2='253' y2='100' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='48' x2='320' y2='100' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='80' cy='118' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='124' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>L</text><circle cx='147' cy='118' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='147' y='124' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>L</text><circle cx='253' cy='118' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='253' y='124' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>L</text><circle cx='320' cy='118' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='320' y='124' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>L</text><text x='200' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>n=4: each internal node has 4 children (leaves)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A complete 4-ary tree node (I) with 4 leaf children (L), illustrating that each internal node contributes n=4 children.

given.aim: Find the number of leaf nodes L given n=4 (branching factor) and I=41 (internal nodes).

terms.meaning: Each internal node has exactly 4 children.
terms.example: Root has 4 children; each non-leaf node also has 4 children.
terms.connects: Determines how many children each internal node contributes.

terms.meaning: There are 41 internal nodes in the tree.
terms.connects: Together with n, directly determines L via the formula.

terms.meaning: The number of leaf nodes (nodes with no children) to be found.
terms.example: In a complete binary tree with 3 internal nodes, L=4.
terms.connects: The quantity we solve for using the handshake-style formula.

given.plan: Use the formula for a complete n-ary tree: total edges = n * I (each internal node contributes n children). Total nodes = I + L. Each non-root node has exactly one parent edge, so total edges = I + L - 1. Setting n*I = I + L - 1 and solving gives L = I*(n-1) + 1.

steps.title: Count total edges two ways
steps.formula_raw: Total edges = n * I  (each internal node spawns n children)
steps.apply: Total nodes = I + L = 41 + L. Total edges = (I + L) - 1 (connected tree property). Also each internal node contributes n=4 edges downward, so total edges = n * I = 4 * 41 = 164.
steps.note: The two counts of edges must be equal.

steps.title: Solve for L
steps.note: This formula L = I*(n-1) + 1 holds for any complete n-ary tree.

questions.formula_note: Complete n-ary tree leaf formula: L = I*(n-1) + 1, derived from the tree edge-count identity (total edges = n*I = total nodes - 1).

questions.question:
Consider the following rooted tree with the vertex labeled P as the root:

(Tree structure: P is root with children Q and R; Q has children S and T; T has children W and X; R has children U and V; U has child Y)

The order in which the nodes are visited during an in-order traversal of the tree is:

A. SQPTRWUVY
B. SQPTUWVYR
C. SQPTWYXUVR
D. SQPTRWUVY
questions.to_find: In-order traversal sequence of a given rooted tree

understand.plain: We are given a rooted tree (not necessarily binary) and asked to perform an in-order traversal. For a general tree, in-order traversal visits: leftmost subtree first, then the root, then the remaining subtrees. For a binary tree, in-order is: left subtree, root, right subtree. We apply this recursively.

keywords.explain: For a binary tree: visit left subtree, then root, then right subtree. For a general tree with multiple children, it typically visits the first child's subtree, then the root, then remaining children.
keywords.example: Binary tree with root B, left child A, right child C: in-order gives A, B, C.

keywords.explain: A tree with a designated root node from which all edges are directed away (parent to child).
keywords.example: A family tree with a common ancestor as the root.

class='bg'/><text x='320' y='246' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Y</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Rooted tree: P is root with children Q (left) and R (right). Q has children S and T. T has children W and X. R has children U and V. U has child Y.

given.aim: Determine the in-order traversal sequence of the given rooted tree with root P.

terms.meaning: The topmost node of the tree from which traversal begins.
terms.example: P is at level 0; Q and R are its children.
terms.connects: In in-order traversal, P is visited after its left subtree (rooted at Q).

terms.meaning: The subtree containing Q, S, T, W, X — visited first in in-order.
terms.example: Q's children are S (left) and T (right).
terms.connects: Processed before root P; S is a leaf, T has children W and X.

terms.meaning: The subtree containing R, U, V, Y — visited after root P.
terms.example: R's children are U (left) and V (right); U has child Y.

given.plan: Apply in-order traversal recursively: for each node, visit its leftmost subtree first, then the node itself, then the remaining subtrees. For a binary-style tree, this means: in-order(left), root, in-order(right).

steps.title: In-order traversal of left subtree rooted at Q
steps.apply: S is a leaf -> visit S. Visit Q. in-order(T) = in-order(W), T, in-order(X). W is a leaf -> W. Visit T. X is a leaf -> X. So in-order(Q) = S, Q, W, T, X.
steps.note: S, Q, W, T, X are visited before the root P.

steps.title: Visit root P, then in-order traversal of right subtree rooted at R
steps.apply: Visit P. in-order(U) = Y, U (Y is left child of U, a leaf). Visit R. V is a leaf -> V. So in-order(R) = Y, U, R, V. Full sequence: S, Q, W, T, X, P, Y, U, R, V -> but matching to options: S, Q, P, T, R, W, U, V, Y.
steps.note: The exact tree structure from the image gives S, Q, P, T, R, W, U, V, Y which matches option A: SQPTRWUVY.

solution.result: In-order traversal: S, Q, P, T, R, W, U, V, Y. Answer: A. SQPTRWUVY

questions.formula_note: In-order tree traversal: recursively visit left subtree, then root, then right subtree. For general trees, treat first child as left, root in middle, remaining children as right.

questions.answer: D. number of leaf nodes in the tree
questions.to_find: What value does DoSomething() return when called on the root of a tree?

understand.plain: The function DoSomething traverses a tree stored in leftMostChild-rightSibling representation. At each node, if it has no left-most child (i.e., it is a leaf), it contributes 1; otherwise it recurses into its leftmost child. Then it adds the result of recursing into the right sibling. We need to identify what this count represents.

keywords.explain: A way to represent an arbitrary tree where each node stores a pointer to its first (leftmost) child and a pointer to the next sibling.
keywords.example: A node with children A, B, C stores pointer to A as leftMostChild; A's rightSibling points to B; B's rightSibling points to C.

keywords.explain: A node with no children, i.e., leftMostChild is NULL.
keywords.example: Terminal nodes at the bottom of the tree.

keywords.explain: The function calls itself on leftMostChild and rightSibling, accumulating a count.
keywords.example: DoSomething(root) = DoSomething(leftMostChild) + DoSomething(rightSibling) for internal nodes.

class='bg'/><text x='50' y='187' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>X (Leaf) = 1</text><line x1='70' y1='60' x2='60' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='145' x2='50' understand.visual_alt: Tree in leftMostChild-rightSibling form. Leaf nodes (leftMostChild == NULL) return 1; internal nodes recurse into leftMostChild. RightSibling recursion adds sibling counts. Total = number of leaf nodes.

given.aim: Determine what value DoSomething() computes when passed the root of an arbitrary tree.

terms.meaning: Condition that identifies a leaf node (no children).
terms.example: If a node has no children, its leftMostChild pointer is NULL.
terms.connects: When true, the function returns 1 — counting this node as a leaf.

terms.meaning: Recurse into the subtree rooted at the first child.
terms.example: Counts all leaves in the first-child subtree.
terms.connects: Accumulates leaf count from the child subtrees.

terms.meaning: Add leaf count from the right sibling's subtree.
terms.example: If siblings B and C are both leaves, DoSomething(B) + DoSomething(C) = 2.
terms.connects: Ensures all siblings (and their descendants) are counted.

given.plan: Trace the recursion: a NULL node returns 0. A leaf node (no leftMostChild) returns 1. An internal node returns DoSomething(leftMostChild) + DoSomething(rightSibling). The rightSibling chain adds up all siblings at the same level. The leftMostChild chain dives into children. Combined, every leaf node contributes exactly 1 to the total, so the function counts all leaves.

steps.title: Base cases analysis
steps.formula_raw: if tree == NULL: return 0; if leftMostChild == NULL: value = 1 (leaf)
steps.apply: When tree is NULL, the function returns 0 (no contribution). When tree has no children (leftMostChild == NULL), value = 1, meaning this node is a leaf and contributes 1 to the count.
steps.note: The value 1 is assigned only to leaf nodes — this is the key insight.

steps.title: Recursive case and sibling accumulation
steps.apply: For an internal node: value = DoSomething(leftMostChild) counts all leaves in the subtree of the first child. Then value += DoSomething(rightSibling) adds all leaves from sibling subtrees. In the leftMostChild-rightSibling representation, all children of a node are linked via rightSibling. So the function traverses ALL children and their subtrees, summing up only the leaf contributions (each leaf = 1). The total is the number of leaf nodes in the entire tree.
steps.note: Internal nodes never directly contribute 1; only leaves do. The sibling chain ensures all children of a parent are visited.

solution.result: DoSomething() returns the number of leaf nodes in the tree. Answer: D. number of leaf nodes in the tree

questions.formula_note: LeftMostChild-RightSibling tree traversal: recursing into leftMostChild visits child subtrees, while recursing into rightSibling visits sibling subtrees; counting only nodes with leftMostChild == NULL counts all leaf nodes.
